ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Kurma Kambing; 'meat stew' (FOODON_03315595) is the closest broader class. A more specific Malay korma term is absent from the current FoodOn release.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP (Meat dish, stewed) is the closest defensible match. No specific Kurma Kambing or Malay korma entry exists in the FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for Kurma Kambing or Malay mutton korma as of 2024.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of Kurma Kambing is approximately 250 g, representing a generous portion of mutton pieces with gravy and potatoes as typically plated at a Malay meal or festive occasion.

nutrient_source.title: Nutrient Composition of Malaysian Foods (MyFCD) — Meat and Poultry Dishes
nutrient_source.organization: Institute for Medical Research (IMR), Ministry of Health Malaysia
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact Kurma Kambing entry was located in MyFCD. Values are adapted from the closest IMR Malaysia MyFCD entries for slow-cooked Malay mutton curry / korma-style dishes and cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for lamb stew with coconut milk. Saturated fat estimated at 6.5 g/100 g based on the coconut milk and mutton fat profile typical of this dish. Monounsaturated, polyunsaturated, trans fat, cholesterol, and water data are not reported in the adapted source and are set to -1.

gi_source.title: International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2021 — analogous estimate for mixed meat-and-potato stew with coconut milk
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Kurma Kambing exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 45 (low) is estimated by analogy with mixed meat-and-potato stews with high fat and protein content, which consistently return GI values in the 40–50 range in the Sydney database. The high fat (13.5 g/100 g) and protein (9.5 g/100 g) content from mutton and coconut milk substantially attenuate postprandial glucose response from the potato carbohydrate component. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (45 × 17.0) / 100 = 7.65, rounded to 7.7.

hypertension.key_factor: Moderate sodium (220 mg/100 g) and high saturated fat from mutton and coconut milk
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 150–200 g, request lean mutton cuts, and avoid adding extra salt, as the combined sodium and saturated fat load can elevate both blood pressure and cardiovascular risk.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~45) attenuated by high protein and fat content
type2_diabetes.guidance: Kurma Kambing has a low estimated GI of 45; the high protein (9.5 g/100 g) and fat (13.5 g/100 g) content slow gastric emptying and blunt postprandial glucose rise, making a 200–250 g portion acceptable for most people with Type 2 Diabetes when total carbohydrate and saturated fat intake for the meal is monitored.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High saturated fat (~6.5 g/100 g) from coconut milk and mutton fat
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The saturated fat content of approximately 6.5 g per 100 g — primarily from coconut milk and mutton — may raise LDL cholesterol with regular consumption; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit servings to 150 g, choose lean cuts, and substitute reduced-fat coconut milk where possible.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory spice profile; moderate saturated fat caveat
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: The aromatic spices (coriander, cumin, cardamom, cinnamon, cloves) used in Kurma Kambing have neutral-to-mild anti-inflammatory properties; however, the high saturated fat from mutton and coconut milk may exacerbate inflammatory markers in some individuals, so portion moderation is advised.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Kurma Kambing is a classic Malay slow-braised mutton (goat or lamb) stew distinguished by its pale, creamy, mildly spiced gravy — a sharp contrast to the fiery red curries common elsewhere in Southeast Asia. The dish is built on a fragrant spice blend of coriander, cumin, fennel, cinnamon, cardamom, and cloves, with no red chillies, giving it a warm, aromatic character without heat. Mutton pieces and potatoes are slow-cooked in a rich base of coconut milk or yogurt, often thickened with ground nuts or fried shallots, producing a velvety, full-bodied sauce. Per 100 g, Kurma Kambing provides approximately 185 kcal, 9.5 g protein, 13.5 g fat (of which an estimated 6.5 g is saturated), and 6.8 g carbohydrate. Its estimated glycemic index of 45 (low) reflects the moderating effect of high protein and fat on carbohydrate digestion. Sodium is moderate at 220 mg/100 g. The dish is a good source of zinc (2.2 mg), iron (1.8 mg), vitamin B12 (1.8 mcg), and niacin (3.2 mg) from the mutton. Kurma Kambing is a staple of Malay festive cuisine, prominently served at weddings (Kenduri) and Hari Raya celebrations, and is typically paired with Nasi Minyak (ghee rice), Nasi Tomato, or plain white rice. It is halal and gluten-free. Clinically, it is suitable in moderate portions for people with Type 2 Diabetes due to its low GI, but its saturated fat content warrants caution for those managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease.

ingredient_synonyms.rempah kurma: kurma spice blend (coriander, cumin, fennel, cinnamon, cardamom, cloves)

confidence_reason: No exact Kurma Kambing entry was found in IMR Malaysia MyFCD or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values are adapted from the closest IMR MyFCD Malay mutton curry / korma-style entries and cross-referenced with USDA SR Legacy lamb stew data. Saturated fat is estimated from the known fat profile of coconut milk and mutton. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for comparable mixed meat-and-potato stews. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation and moisture data for Kurma Kambing are not reported in IMR Malaysia MyFCD or USDA FoodData Central for this specific preparation as of 2024. 2. saturated_fat_g (6.5 g/100 g) is estimated from the known saturated fat content of coconut milk (~85% of coconut milk fat is saturated) and typical mutton fat composition; this value should be confirmed against a direct laboratory analysis or a future MyFCD entry. 3. GI of 45 is an analogous estimate; a direct GI measurement for Kurma Kambing has not been published in the University of Sydney GI Database as of 2024. 4. The input source cited 'Ministry of Health Malaysia' at moh.gov.my; this has been mapped to the IMR Malaysia MyFCD database (myfcd.moh.gov.my), which is the approved nutrient composition database under MOH Malaysia. 5. All core macros (protein_g 9.5, fat_g 13.5, carbohydrate_g 6.8) are non-zero and consistent with a mutton-and-coconut-milk stew; no zero-macro justification is required.
